license: License not yet finalized — contact the repository maintainer before redistributing. Underlying court decisions are public record; editorial summaries, takeaways, and dramatizations are original to this project; verbatim quotes remain the work of their original authors.

evidence_tier.name: Cited
evidence_tier.description: Real, adjudicated IDEA case law. Every episode is grounded in an actual court or administrative decision, with citation and source URL. Facts, rules, and holdings are real; dramatization dialogue is invented composite illustration of those real facts.

disclaimers: This dataset is educational reference material, not legal advice. Consult a qualified special-education attorney or advocate for any specific situation.

All 'dramatization' dialogue is invented composite illustration written for readability. Speaker names are fictional. Facts, citations, legal holdings, and verbatim quotes are real; the dialogue is not.

categories.name: Predetermination
categories.description: The team decides the outcome before the meeting, instead of weighing the child's actual evidence.

categories.name: Missing Required Team Member
categories.description: A legally required role is missing from the IEP team, regardless of what the plan itself says.

categories.name: Parent Participation
categories.description: The parent isn't meaningfully included in the decisions, only informed of them afterward.

categories.name: Right to Challenge (Notice)
categories.description: Parents are never told they have the right to formally challenge the plan.

categories.name: "Stay Put" Rights
categories.description: What happens to the child's placement while a disagreement is still being resolved.

categories.name: Child Find / Duty to Evaluate
categories.description: The school fails to evaluate a suspected disability that's already been directly raised.

categories.name: Retaliation Against Parents
categories.description: The school responds to a parent asserting their rights with punitive action.

categories.name: Burden of Proof in Disputes
categories.description: Who has to prove the plan is wrong once a dispute reaches a formal hearing.

categories.name: Understandable Language
categories.description: A parent isn't given information in a language or format they can actually understand, undermining everything that follows.

categories.name: Independent Educational Evaluation
categories.description: The school fails to honor a parent's right to have their child evaluated by an independent, outside evaluator at public expense.

categories.name: Records Access
categories.description: The school withholds a parent's access to their child's own evaluation records, undermining everything downstream.

source.case: Deal v. Hamilton County Board of Education

episodes.facts: The school district had an unofficial policy of refusing to consider intensive Lovaas-style ABA therapy for autistic students, regardless of the individual child's needs or the evidence from his private program. The IEP team meeting went forward, but the administrative law judge found 191 facts showing the outcome had already been decided before anyone sat down.
episodes.rule: Predetermining an outcome before the IEP meeting - deciding based on a blanket policy instead of the child's individual evidence - is itself a procedural violation of IDEA, independent of whether the outcome would otherwise have been appropriate. It deprives the parents of meaningful participation, which the court found was enough on its own to deny a FAPE.
episodes.takeaway: If the team's mind was made up before the meeting started, it doesn't matter how the meeting goes from there - that's already a legal violation.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We've reviewed everything, and per our program model, we don't provide intensive one-on-one ABA services in this district. That's just not something we offer.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: You haven't asked anything about what's actually worked for him. He's been in a home ABA program for over a year with real progress.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: For what it's worth, that reads to me like a policy decision from above us, not something based on his individual file.

source.case: M.L. v. Federal Way School District

episodes.facts: The IEP team met and developed a plan without a regular education teacher present, even though the student spent part of his day in a general education classroom.
episodes.rule: IDEA requires at least one regular education teacher on the IEP team whenever the child is, or may be, participating in the general education environment. The court called the omission a "structural defect" serious enough that it precluded even asking whether the resulting IEP was adequate - the team was missing the expertise on general curriculum and environment that role is meant to provide.
episodes.takeaway: Who's required to be in the room isn't a formality - a legally required role missing from the table can invalidate the whole plan, independent of what the plan actually says.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: Wait - is anyone here from his regular classroom? He's mainstreamed for half the day.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We didn't think that was necessary for this particular meeting.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: Actually, IDEA requires a regular ed teacher on the team whenever the student may participate in general ed - that's not something we can skip.

source.case: Doug C. v. Hawaii Department of Education

episodes.facts: The agency had trouble scheduling a meeting with the father and proceeded to finalize the IEP without him. He was informed of the completed plan afterward rather than being part of developing it.
episodes.rule: The Ninth Circuit held that scheduling difficulty does not excuse proceeding without a parent who has expressed willingness to participate - an agency "cannot eschew its affirmative duties under IDEA by blaming the parents." Briefing a parent on an IEP after it's already adopted does not satisfy the participation requirement.
episodes.takeaway: The parent has to be there before decisions are made, not briefed on them after. 'We tried to find a time' isn't a legal exception if the meeting happens anyway.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We had a hard time finding a date that worked with your schedule, so we went ahead and finalized the IEP. I can walk you through it with you now.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I told you I was willing to meet - I just needed about a week's notice. Nobody followed up.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: Going over it with you now honestly isn't the same as you being part of deciding it in the first place.

source.case: Jaynes v. Newport News Public Schools
source.citation: 13 Fed. Appx. 166 (4th Cir. 2001) (unpublished), affirming an E.D. Va. judgment following a Virginia due-process hearing officer decision (June 11, 1999)

episodes.facts: A family with an autistic child providing intensive home-based ABA/Lovaas services was never informed of their right to a due process hearing to challenge the school's IEP. The mother had signed a "consent to testing" form without being told about her broader parental rights in the process.
episodes.rule: The hearing officer found the school's repeated failure to notify the parents of their right to challenge the IEP through due process was itself a procedural violation that denied FAPE - the family had "seriously infringed" participation rights. A due process hearing officer initially awarded the family $117,979.78 in legal and educational reimbursement costs; after a state-level reduction, the district court entered final judgment of roughly $103,000, which the Fourth Circuit affirmed in 2001.
episodes.takeaway: Getting a form to sign isn't the same as being told your rights. If nobody mentions you can formally challenge this, that silence is the violation - and it can get expensive for the district.

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Novak
dramatization.line: Here's the consent form for testing - if you could just sign here, we can get started.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: Before I sign - what exactly am I agreeing to, and what are my options if I disagree with the results later?

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Novak
dramatization.line: Let's just get the testing scheduled first and we can sort out the rest later.

source.case: Burlington School Committee v. Massachusetts Dept. of Education

episodes.facts: Parents disagreed with the school's proposed IEP and unilaterally moved their child to a different placement while the dispute was still pending, ahead of any final ruling.
episodes.rule: The Supreme Court held (9-0) that a parent moving a child during a pending dispute does not automatically forfeit reimbursement rights - courts retain authority to order retroactive reimbursement if the private placement is later found appropriate. Reimbursement is only barred for the interim period if the original IEP is ultimately found adequate.
episodes.takeaway: Stay-put protects the status quo during a dispute, but acting to protect your kid mid-disagreement doesn't automatically cost you your rights if you're later proven right.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I don't agree with this placement. I'm moving him to a program I trust while we get this sorted out.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: If you do that against our recommendation, you could end up losing any right to be reimbursed for it later.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: That's not quite right, actually - courts have held that doesn't automatically waive reimbursement if the placement is later found appropriate.

source.case: Phyllene W. v. Huntsville City Board of Education

episodes.facts: The parent twice told the school her daughter had hearing loss - once at the end of fifth grade, and again during the tenth-grade IEP meeting, when she said her daughter would soon be fitted for a hearing aid. The school never followed up or evaluated her for it.
episodes.rule: The Eleventh Circuit found the district violated IDEA's Child Find duty by failing to evaluate despite repeated, direct notice from the parent that her daughter had hearing loss. Without that evaluation, no meaningful IEP could be built - the court found the resulting IEPs categorically lacked the services she needed.
episodes.takeaway: If a parent or teacher directly raises a concern, 'let's keep monitoring' isn't a neutral holding pattern - it's the specific failure IDEA calls Child Find, and it can void the IEPs built without the missing evaluation.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I raised this with you back in fifth grade, and I'm raising it again now - she's about to be fitted for a hearing aid.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: Let's keep an eye on it and revisit next semester if it's still a concern.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: This is the second time she's raised it - that's exactly the pattern that's been found to violate Child Find. We should be evaluating now, not waiting again.

source.case: A.C. v. Shelby County Board of Education

episodes.facts: After a parent formally asserted their child's Section 504 rights, a school principal responded by filing false child-abuse allegations against the parents.
episodes.rule: The Sixth Circuit held that schools cannot take adverse action against parents in retaliation for asserting disability-rights protections. A retaliation claim can proceed independently of whatever the underlying services dispute was about.
episodes.takeaway: Asserting your rights is protected activity. If the response to a parent pushing for services is punitive rather than substantive, that's its own separate legal problem - not just an unpleasant meeting.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I'm formally requesting the accommodations we discussed be added to his file in writing.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: Understood. Separately, I do want to mention some concerns have come up about your household that we may need to look into.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: ...I don't think that's related to what she just asked for.

episodes.facts: Parents disagreed with an IEP and requested a due process hearing, working from the common assumption (accurate in most jurisdictions before this case) that the school district would have to justify its own IEP by default.
episodes.rule: The Supreme Court held that the burden of proof at a due process hearing challenging an IEP falls on whichever party brought the challenge - usually the parent - not on the school district by default. This was a significant shift from the prior common practice in most jurisdictions.
episodes.takeaway: If you're the one challenging the plan, you're the one who has to prove it's wrong. Walking into a hearing assuming the district has to justify itself first is a real, common misunderstanding worth correcting before you're in the room.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I disagree with this evaluation, so I want to request a hearing - you'll need to show why your version is correct.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: Actually, once you request the hearing, the legal burden is on you to show the IEP is inappropriate, not on us to defend it by default.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I didn't realize that. That changes how I need to prepare for this.

source.case: Parent on Behalf of Student v. Bellflower Unified School District
source.citation: Cal. Office of Administrative Hearings, Case No. 2016100887 (2017)

episodes.facts: The district knew the mother was a Spanish speaker with very limited English - she needed help completing English-language enrollment forms, made her initial written evaluation request in Spanish, and had documented difficulty communicating with staff in English. Despite this, the district never provided Spanish translations of three separate IEP documents, and failed to provide an interpreter - or ensure all necessary team members were present - at the IEP team meeting where the most recent plan was finalized.
episodes.rule: The administrative law judge found the district significantly impeded the mother's opportunity to participate in the IEP process. Providing an interpreter at one brief meeting plus an English-only copy of the IEP did not give her the information necessary to understand and participate. On these facts, her consent to the IEPs was found not - and could not reasonably have been interpreted as - informed.
episodes.takeaway: Consent isn't 'informed' just because a form got signed. If a parent genuinely can't read or understand the IEP in a language they know, an interpreter at one meeting plus an English-only document doesn't satisfy that - and it can undo the informed-consent basis for the whole plan.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We have an interpreter here for today's meeting, and I'll get you a copy of the IEP afterward.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: The last two IEPs were only in English. I couldn't read them, and there was no one to explain what I was agreeing to.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: An interpreter for one meeting and an English-only document afterward isn't really enough for you to have understood what you signed.

source.citation: Pennsylvania due-process hearing decisions (Office for Dispute Resolution, hearing-officer decision number not independently located); Y.C.Q. v. Chichester School District, No. 25-2788 (3d Cir., appeal pending as of Nov. 2025)

episodes.facts: Y.C.Q., a high-school student in foster care and an English learner with a disability, had her request for an independent educational evaluation effectively denied and delayed by the district. Following two due-process hearings, a hearing officer found she had been unlawfully deprived of special education services and ordered the district to pay for an independent educational evaluation and to create an IEP for her. The district refused to comply, and the dispute escalated to federal court, where the district court denied Y.C.Q. stay-put protections and blocked the state education department from enforcing the hearing officer's IEE order. Y.C.Q. and her family appealed to the Third Circuit, where the case remained pending as of late 2025.
episodes.rule: When a hearing officer determines a district's own evaluation was inadequate, the district can be ordered to fund an independent educational evaluation at public expense directly. Refusing to comply doesn't make that obligation go away - it can escalate into federal litigation, as it did here, where the case is still unresolved on appeal.
episodes.takeaway: If a district stalls or refuses an IEE request, a hearing officer can order the district to pay for one outright. And if the district still won't comply, that's an enforceable violation you can escalate - not a dead end, even though this particular fight shows escalation can take years to fully resolve.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I don't agree with the district's evaluation. I'm requesting an independent evaluation at public expense.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We'll take that under consideration and get back to you.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: Once you've made that request, the district has to either fund it or file for a hearing to defend its own evaluation without unnecessary delay - 'we'll consider it' isn't really a third option.

source.case: Amanda J. v. Clark County School District

episodes.facts: The district evaluated a young child for developmental delays. Its own school psychologist and speech pathologist identified strong indicators of autism - the speech pathologist rated her 'severely autistic' on diagnostic scales. When the parents requested her evaluation records, the district gave them only a two-page summary that omitted any mention of autism or a recommendation for psychiatric evaluation. The parents didn't see the full reports until months later, after moving out of state and requesting a file transfer.
episodes.rule: The Ninth Circuit held that withholding the complete evaluation records from the parents was itself a procedural IDEA violation - parents can't meaningfully participate in decisions about a child's disability if the district doesn't give them the full picture of what its own evaluators found. The court held this alone denied FAPE: 'By failing to disclose [the child's] full records to her parents once they were requested... the District denied [her] a FAPE.'
episodes.takeaway: A two-page summary isn't the same as the actual evaluation. If a district's own evaluators found something significant and it doesn't show up in what you're handed, that's not just an incomplete file - it's the specific violation that can undo the process.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: I requested her full evaluation file last week. This is only two pages - is that everything?

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Novak
dramatization.line: That's a summary of the key findings, should be enough to go on for today's meeting.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: If there's more in the full evaluation than what's summarized here, she's entitled to see all of it before we make decisions based on it.

episodes.facts: Parents enrolled their daughter in a private school and sought reimbursement; a hearing officer agreed in 2009 and set that private placement as her stay-put placement. The district appealed. By the time the Third Circuit ruled in 2012, it reversed on the underlying IEP claim - but the parents still sought reimbursement for the stay-put placement's cost for the entire period the appeal was pending, 2009-2012.
episodes.rule: The Third Circuit held IDEA's stay-put provision (20 U.S.C. section 1415(j)) extends through judicial appeals, not just administrative proceedings - the obligation to fund a child's stay-put placement continues through final resolution at every level, including the courts.
episodes.takeaway: Stay-put doesn't expire when a case moves from a hearing officer to a courtroom. If a placement was your child's stay-put placement at the start of a dispute, the district's obligation to fund it can keep running through the entire appeals process, however long that takes.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: The hearing officer's stay-put order was from the original decision - now that we're appealing, our funding obligation is on hold until this is resolved.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: That's not how I understood stay-put to work - I thought his placement stays funded the whole time this is being fought over.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: She's right - stay-put runs through the full appeals process, not just up to the first ruling. That funding doesn't pause because you're appealing.

episodes.facts: After one meeting with the principal before her son started school, a mother spent his first and second grade years (1991-1993) repeatedly raising documented concerns with school officials about his educational difficulties, asking that he be evaluated. The school persistently refused to evaluate, classify, or provide any services.
episodes.rule: The Third Circuit held that damages are available under IDEA, Section 504, and Section 1983 when a district refuses to evaluate, classify, and serve a child despite a parent's repeated, documented requests - a district can't simply run out the clock on Child Find by ignoring a parent. (The Section 1983 route specifically was later closed off: the Third Circuit abrogated that part of this holding in A.W. v. Jersey City Public Schools, 486 F.3d 791 (3d Cir. 2007), following the Supreme Court's Rancho Palos Verdes v. Abrams - Section 1983 is no longer available for IDEA/504 claims in this circuit, though direct damages under Section 504 remain available.)
episodes.takeaway: Child Find isn't satisfied by waiting the parent out. If you've raised the concern more than once and the school still won't evaluate, that pattern of refusal is itself actionable - not just an ongoing frustration.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: This is the third time I'm asking - can we please get him evaluated? I've been raising this since the start of the year.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We appreciate your patience, we just don't think an evaluation is warranted right now.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: Repeated requests like this over months without an evaluation is exactly the pattern that's been found to violate Child Find - this shouldn't keep getting deferred.

source.case: H.P. v. Board of Education of the City of Chicago

episodes.facts: Seven Chicago Public Schools students with IEPs, and their Limited-English-Proficient parents (primarily Spanish, Mandarin, and Polish speakers), brought a class action alleging CPS had no policy for providing written translations of IEP documents, no independent interpreters at IEP meetings, and used untrained ad hoc staff as interpreters with no competency evaluation - resulting in incomplete, inaccurate, editorialized translations district-wide.
episodes.rule: The court denied the district's motion to dismiss, holding the allegations were sufficient to show LEP parents were denied meaningful language access, which can itself constitute denial of the child's IDEA rights - the lack of translation/interpretation significantly impeded parents' ability to participate in the IEP process.
episodes.takeaway: This isn't just a one-family problem - a district-wide pattern of ad hoc, unverified interpretation is itself an actionable systemic violation, not just bad luck in one meeting.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: Every time we have a meeting, whoever happens to be free interprets for me. Nobody's ever asked if they're actually qualified to do it.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: It's whoever's available that day - we don't have a formal interpreter program.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: That's not just your situation - if the district has no real standard for this, that's a pattern that can violate every LEP family's rights, not just yours.

source.case: Alta Loma School District IEE Delay Dispute
source.citation: Cal. Office of Administrative Hearings, Case No. 2017120979 (2019, decision after partial remand from L.C. v. Alta Loma School Dist., 2019 WL 3246505 (C.D. Cal.))

episodes.facts: A parent requested public funding for an independent visual-processing evaluation from a named evaluator, whose rate of roughly $2,400 was well over the SELPA's $900 cost criteria. The district didn't clearly communicate that gap to the parent, who went ahead and independently paid for and obtained the evaluation before the district agreed to fund it - an unnecessary 84-day delay (September to December 2017) in the district's own response. OAH's original 2018 decision found the district hadn't unreasonably delayed, but a federal district court partially reversed that finding in 2019 and sent the case back.
episodes.rule: On remand, the ALJ held that the district's 84-day delay in clearly communicating the cost-criteria gap was itself a substantive denial of FAPE, significantly interfering with the parent's participation in the process - even though the district hadn't refused the request outright, and even though no additional remedy was ordered beyond the evaluation itself.
episodes.takeaway: A district doesn't have to refuse an IEE request outright to violate this right. Sitting on it for weeks without clearly telling you what's actually holding things up - like a cost gap you're never told about - can be found to deny FAPE on its own, even if an evaluation eventually gets funded or you end up paying for it yourself in the meantime.

dramatization.speaker: Dana Reyes
dramatization.line: It's been weeks since I asked for an independent evaluation and I still don't have a straight answer on the cost.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Whitfield
dramatization.line: We're still working out whether the evaluator's rate is within our standard cost criteria - we'll let you know.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
dramatization.line: Sitting on that answer for weeks without telling her how far off the rate is from our own criteria isn't a neutral delay - that's exactly the kind of thing that's been found to deny FAPE on its own.
